author: Thucydides
title: Ἱστορίαι
original_text: κατὰ γῆν δὲ πόλεμος, ὅθεν τις καὶ δύναμις παρεγένετο, οὐδεὶς ξυνέστη· πάντες δὲ ἦσαν, ὅσοι καὶ ἐγένοντο, πρὸς ὁμόρους τοὺς σφετέρους ἑκάστοις, καὶ ἐκδήμους στρατείας πολὺ ἀπὸ τῆς ἑαυτῶν ἐπ’ ἄλλων καταστροφῇ οὐκ ἐξῇσαν οἱ Ἕλληνες. οὐ γὰρ ξυνειστήκεσαν πρὸς τὰς μεγίστας πόλεις ὑπήκοοι, οὐδ’ αὖ αὐτοὶ ἀπὸ τῆς ἴσης κοινὰς στρατείας ἐποιοῦντο, κατ’ ἀλλήλους δὲ μᾶλλον ὡς ἕκαστοι οἱ ἀστυγείτονες ἐπολέμουν. μάλιστα δὲ ἐς τὸν πάλαι ποτὲ γενόμενον πόλεμον Χαλκιδέων καὶ Ἐρετριῶν καὶ τὸ ἄλλο Ἑλληνικὸν ἐς ξυμ

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Concerning land warfare, no unified command arose, and all Greeks, as they had previously, operated against their immediate neighbors. The Greeks did not undertake large-scale campaigns as a collective force, nor were they subject to the larger cities, but rather each city waged war against its closest neighbors. This situation resembled the earlier conflict between the Chalcidians and Eretrians, and the broader Hellenic alliances were divided. Furthermore, obstacles prevented growth elsewhere; when Cyrus and the Persian kings, after overthrowing Croesus, campaigned beyond the Halys River towards the sea and subjugated the cities on the mainland, and later Darius controlled the Phoenician fleet and the islands, this created further divisions. Tyrants within the Greek cities, focused solely on their own power, sought to increase their personal wealth and security as much as possible, and they did not accomplish any noteworthy deeds except in conflicts with their local neighbors. Those in Sicily, in particular, expanded their power. Thus, throughout Greece, for a long period, there was no collective action, and cities were hesitant to act boldly.
gemma3:27b.rationale: This is a direct rendering of the passage’s claims about the fragmented political and military landscape of Greece. No terms related to water infrastructure or Nile Valley administration are present.

author: Diogenes Laertius
title: Βίοι καὶ γνῶμαι τῶν ἐν φιλοσοφίᾳ εὐδοκιμησάντων
original_text: Οἱ δὲ τὴν εὕρεσιν διδόντες ἐκείνοις παράγουσι καὶ Ὀρφέα τὸν Θρᾷκα, λέγοντες φιλόσοφον γεγονέναι καὶ εἶναι ἀρχαιότατον. ἐγὼ δέ, εἰ τὸν περὶ θεῶν ἐξαγορεύσαντα τοιαῦτα χρὴ φιλόσοφον καλεῖν οὐκ οἶδα, 〈οὐδὲ〉 οὐδὲ addidit Apelt. τίνα δεῖ προσαγορεύειν τὸν πᾶν τὸ ἀνθρώπειον πάθος ἀφειδοῦντα τοῖς θεοῖς προστρῖψαι, καὶ τὰ σπανίως ὑπό τινων ἀνθρώπων αἰσχρουργούμενα τῷ τῆς φωνῆς ὀργάνῳ. τοῦτον δὲ ὁ μὲν μῦθος ὑπὸ γυναικῶν ἀπολέσθαι φησί· τὸ δʼ ἐν Δίῳ τῆς Μακεδονίας ἐπίγραμμα, κεραυνωθῆναι αὐτόν, λέγον οὕτω

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Those who claim to have originated this [philosophical inquiry] also cite Orpheus of Thrace, stating that he was a philosopher and a very ancient figure. However, I do not know whether one should call someone a philosopher who speaks about the gods in such a manner. What should one call someone who spares no human passion, attributing it to the gods, and expressing through vocal performance things that are rarely and shamefully done by some humans? Myth says this Orpheus met his end at the hands of women. An epitaph in Dion, in Macedonia, states that he was struck by lightning, with the following words: ‘The Muses buried Orpheus the Thracian golden lyre player, whom high-throned Zeus killed with a blazing arrow.’ Some claim that philosophy originated among barbarians, and they detail its method as practiced by each group. They say that the Gymnosophists and Druids philosophized by speaking in riddles, revering the gods, refraining from evil deeds, and practicing courage. Clearchus, in his twelfth book, states that the Gymnosophists also disregarded death. The Chaldeans, they say, devoted themselves to astronomy and prediction. The Magi, they say, devoted themselves to divine healing, sacrifices, and prayers, claiming to be the only ones heard by the gods. They also spoke about the essence and origin of the gods, identifying them as fire, earth, and water, and were knowledgeable about idols, especially those representing gods as male and female.
gemma3:27b.rationale: This passage is primarily framing a historical argument about the origins of philosophy. qwen3:32b.cot_english: Diogenes Laertius discusses the origins of philosophy, debating whether the Thracian Orpheus should be considered a philosopher. He argues that if someone who revealed divine truths qualifies as a philosopher, it is unclear how to label someone who abandoned all human passions for the gods and used the voice's instrument to perform rare human acts. Myth claims Orpheus was killed by women, while an epigram in Dium, Macedonia, states Zeus struck him with a thunderbolt, burying him with the Muses. The text then examines non-Greek groups as early philosophers: Gymnosophists and Druids, who used riddles to teach reverence for gods, avoiding evil, and cultivating courage. Gymnosophists, according to Kleitarchos in Book 12, disregarded death. Chaldeans focused on astronomy and prophecy, while Magi specialized in divine rituals, sacrifices, and prayers, claiming to be the only ones heard. They also discussed divine substance, asserting gods are fire, earth, and water, and rejected idols, especially those depicting gods as male or female.
qwen3:32b.rationale: The passage centers on philosophical origins and non-Greek groups' practices, with no direct references to water infrastructure, environmental terms, or Nile Valley administration. The vocab_terms list lacks relevant entries for this context.

author: Julius Caesar
title: De bello Gallico
original_text: Gallia est omnis divisa in partes tres, quarum unam incolunt Belgae, aliam Aquitani, tertiam qui ipsorum lingua Celtae, nostra Galli appellantur. Hi omnes lingua, institutis, legibus inter se differunt. Gallos ab Aquitanis Garumna flumen, a Belgis Matrona et Sequana dividit. Horum omnium fortissimi sunt Belgae, propterea quod a cultu atque humanitate provinciae longissime absunt, minimeque ad eos mercatores saepe commeant atque ea quae ad effeminandos animos pertinent important, proximique sunt

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Julius Caesar states that all Gaul is divided into three parts: the Belgae, the Aquitani, and the people who, in their own language, are called Celts, but are called Gauls by the Romans. These groups all differ in language, customs, and laws. The Garumna River divides the Gauls from the Aquitani, and the Matrona and Sequana Rivers divide them from the Belgae. The Belgae are the strongest of all these peoples because they are located farthest from the civilization and refinement of the Roman province, few merchants trade with them (importing things that lead to effeminacy), and they are closest to the Germans who live across the Rhine River, with whom they are constantly at war. Because of this, the Helvetii also surpass the other Gauls in courage, as they frequently fight with the Germans, either preventing them from crossing their borders or waging war in German territory. One part of Gaul, said to be inhabited by the Gauls, begins at the Rhodanus River, is contained by the Garumna River, the Ocean, and the borders of the Belgae, and also reaches the Rhine River from the Sequani and Helvetii, extending towards the north. The Belgae originate from the farthest borders of Gaul, extend to the lower part of the Rhine River, and face the rising and setting sun (north and east). Aquitania extends from the Garumna River to the Pyrenees Mountains and that part of the Ocean bordering Spain, facing between the setting sun and the north. Among the Helvetii, Orgetorix was the most noble and wealthy. Driven by a desire for kingship during the consulship of Marcus Messalla and Publius Piso, he formed a conspiracy with the nobility and persuaded the state to leave their territories with all their forces.
gemma3:27b.rationale: This is a straightforward descriptive passage. The translation prioritizes clear identification of groups and geographic features. All content is directly attested in the text; no inference or framing is necessary.

qwen3:32b.cot_english: Julius Caesar's *De bello Gallico* describes the division of Gaul into three regions: the Belgae inhabit one part, the Aquitani another, and the third is occupied by people called Celtae by their own language and Galli by the Romans. These groups differ in language, customs, and laws. The Garumna River separates the Gauls from the Aquitani, while the Matrona and Sequana Rivers divide them from the Belgae. The Belgae are the strongest due to their distance from Roman civilization, minimal trade contact, and proximity to the Germanic tribes across the Rhine with whom they frequently wage war. The Helvetii, another group, are considered the most virtuous among the Gauls because of their constant battles with the Germans, either defending their territory or invading German lands. The region controlled by the Gauls begins at the Rhodanus River, is bounded by the Garumna, the ocean, and the Belgae, and extends to the Rhine near the Sequani and Helvetii. The Belgae occupy the northernmost parts of Gaul, near the lower Rhine, facing north and east. The Aquitani region lies between the Garumna River, the Pyrenees Mountains, and the Atlantic Ocean near Hispania, facing west and north. The most prominent and wealthy leader among the Helvetii was Orgetorix, who, during the consulship of M. Messala and M. Pisones, conspired with the nobility to persuade the Helvetii to migrate with all their forces from their homeland.

author: M. Tullius Cicero
title: Pro S. Roscio Amerino
original_text: quid ergo? audacissimus ego ex omnibus? minime . an An Schol. : at (ac V ω ) codd. tanto officiosior quam ceteri? ne istius quidem laudis ita sum sum sim V ω cupidus ut aliis eam praereptam velim. quae me igitur res praeter ceteros impulit ut causam Sex. Rosci reciperem? quia , si qui istorum dixisset quos videtis adesse, in quibus summa auctoritas est atque amplitudo, si verbum de re publica fecisset, id quod in hac causa fieri necesse est, multo plura dixisse quam dixisset putaretur. ego autem

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Cicero argues that he is not being overly bold in defending Sextus Roscius, but rather is acting with a level of public service comparable to other prominent Romans. He does not seek praise for taking on this case, but explains that he was compelled to do so because individuals of high standing, were they to speak about matters of state – as is necessary in this case – would speak at greater length than they might intend. Cicero anticipates criticism that his speech might be too free-spoken, but asserts that it will not reach the general public in the same way as the speeches of established figures. Furthermore, others are constrained by their status and age, preventing them from speaking rashly. If Cicero speaks more freely, it may be excused as the behavior of a young man who has not yet fully entered public life, though the custom of leniency towards youth is diminishing.

author: Plutarch
title: Πῶς δεῖ τὸν νέον ποιημάτων ἀκούειν.
original_text: εἰ μέν, ὡς Φιλόξενυς ὁ ποιητὴς ἔλεγεν, ὦ Μάρκε Σήδατε, Σήδατε Basileensis: σήδαπε τῶν κρεῶν τὰ μὴ κρέα ἥδιστά ἐστι καὶ τῶν ἰχθύων οἱ μὴ ἰχθύες, ἐκείνοις ἀποφαίνεσθαι παρῶμεν οἷς ὁ Κάτων ἔφη τῆς καρδίας τὴν ὑπερῴαν εὐαισθητοτέραν ὑπάρχειν. ὅτι δὲ τῶν ἐν φιλοσοφίᾳ λεγομένων οἱ σφόδρα νέοι τοῖς μὴ δοκοῦσι φιλοσόφως μηδʼ ἀπὸ σπουδῆς λέγεσθαι χαίρουσι μᾶλλον καὶ παρέχουσιν ὑπηκόους ἑαυτοὺς καὶ χειροήθεις, δῆλόν ἐστιν ἡμῖν. οὐ γὰρ μόνον τὰ Αἰσώπεια μυθάρια καὶ τὰς ποιητικὰς ὑποθέσεις ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸν Ἄβαρ

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Plutarch discusses how young people should approach poetry. He argues, as the poet Philoxenus stated to Marcus Sedatus, that just as some parts of a meal are more enjoyable than the meat itself and some fish are more palatable than the fish, so too should we focus on what appeals to the discerning mind – the part of the heart that Cato believed was most sensitive. Young people readily enjoy philosophical ideas presented not as serious study but as pleasant entertainment, readily becoming followers and eager assistants. We see this in their enjoyment of Aesop’s fables, poetic narratives, the stories of Abaris of Hyperboria, and Lycon of Aristion, even when these incorporate myths about the soul. Therefore, we must cultivate self-control not only in pleasures of food and drink, but even more so in listening and reading, moderating their exposure to enjoyable things to pursue what is useful and beneficial. Just as a city with open gates is vulnerable if it allows enemies to enter, so too is a young person’s self-control undermined if, through unchecked listening, they allow harmful ideas to take root. The more naturally inclined someone is to thinking and reasoning, the more easily harmful ideas can take hold and corrupt them. Therefore, since it is neither possible nor beneficial to completely shield someone like young Socrates and Cleander from poetry, we must carefully monitor their reading, recognizing that they need guidance more than strict pedagogy. I have written these thoughts on poetry and will send them to you for review. If you find them no more objectionable than the libations poured during drinking, share them with Cleander, recognizing his energetic and receptive nature. The polypus, though harmful, is also pleasurable to taste, causing disturbing and bizarre dreams, as some say. Similarly, poetry contains much that is nourishing to a young mind, but also much that is disruptive and overwhelming if not accompanied by proper instruction. Just as there are medicines that, while beneficial when properly mixed, can be harmful when taken carelessly, so too does poetry offer both pleasure and danger. There is a fondness, a passion, and a deceptive persuasion that can steal the minds even of the wise. Deception does not affect the foolish or unintelligent. Simonides famously responded to someone who questioned why he didn’t deceive anyone by saying they were too unintelligent to be deceived. Gorgias even called tragedy a form of deception, arguing that the deceiver is more just than the one not deceived, and the deceived is wiser than the one not deceived. Should we, like the inhabitants of Ithaca, forcibly mold the ears of young people with harsh and inflexible wax, forcing them to reject poetry, or should we, with sound judgment and moderation, guide their critical faculties so that pleasure does not lead to harm? Even Lycoargus, the strong son of Dryas, did not possess a sound mind, for while intoxicated he cut down the vines instead of directing the springs closer. Plato says this represents a misguided attempt to control a frenzied god. Wine removes harm by not cooperating with what is beneficial. Therefore, let us not cut off the daily feast of the Muses, but rather, when poetic inspiration becomes unrestrained, arrogant, and delusional, let us intervene and restrain it. When poetry is graced by the Muses and its language is sweet, instructive, and fruitful, let us introduce philosophy and blend the two. Just as mandrake growing near vines strengthens the wine, making it smoother for those who drink it, so too does poetry, infused with philosophy, make learning lighter and more appealing to young people. Therefore, poetry should not be avoided by those who wish to philosophize, but rather, those who engage with poetry should first philosophize, seeking and loving the useful within the pleasurable. If not, they will struggle and be frustrated. This is the beginning of education, and as Sophocles said, a good beginning usually leads to a good end. The polypus, though harmful, is also pleasurable; many medicines are beneficial when mixed, but harmful when taken carelessly. There is fondness, passion, and a deceptive persuasion that can steal the minds even of the wise.
gemma3:27b.rationale: This is a complex philosophical argument, so the 'framing' classification is appropriate. The translation prioritizes conveying the overall argument and supporting claims, rather than focusing on specific details related to water management. The passage does not contain any terms from the provided vocabulary lists.

He warns that unguarded exposure to poetic works—like unfiltered wine or deceptive drugs—can harm a youth's rational judgment. Plutarch advocates for a balanced approach: using philosophical reasoning to extract utility from poetry while avoiding its harmful aspects. He emphasizes that poetry, when properly filtered through education, can soften harsh truths and make learning accessible, but unregulated consumption leads to moral and intellectual decay. The passage addresses Marcus Sceva and his son Cleander, urging them to treat poetry as a tool for disciplined education rather than mere entertainment.

original_text: etsi tibi omnia suppetunt ea quae consequi ingenio aut usu homines aut intelligentia possunt, tamen amore nostro non sum alienum arbitratus ad te perscribere ea quae mihi veniebant in mentem dies ac noctes de petitione tua cogitanti, non ut aliquid ex his novi addisceres sed ut ea quae in re dispersa atque infinita viderentur esse ratione et distributione sub uno aspectu ponerentur. civitas quae sit cogita, quid petas, qui sis. prope cotidie tibi hoc ad forum descendenti meditandum est ' novus s

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Cicero advises a candidate for the consulship, even though the candidate possesses all the qualities – talent, experience, and intelligence – necessary for success. He writes to offer advice on how to organize thoughts and present a coherent case for the office, not to provide new information. The candidate must constantly reflect on the nature of the Roman state, the office being sought, and his own position. When appearing before the public forum, the candidate should emphasize his newness and his pursuit of the consulship, stating ‘I am new, I am seeking the consulship, this is Rome.’ Highlighting this novelty will generate prestige, as a patron who supports capable consuls is not seen as supporting an unworthy candidate. Therefore, the candidate should approach the campaign as if every interaction is a judgment of his overall character. He should prepare the resources he already possesses – his network of publicans (tax collectors), the equestrian order, local municipalities, those he has defended, and various associations – and cultivate the support of young men eager to learn. He has advantages few new candidates have. He must actively remind these groups of their support and ensure they understand there will be no other opportunity to assist him. The goodwill of prominent citizens, especially former consuls, is also crucial; it is beneficial to be seen by them as worthy of the position and its associated influence. He should diligently seek their support, emphasizing that he and his allies have always prioritized the interests of the aristocracy and avoided popularist policies. Any instances of seemingly populist rhetoric should be explained as strategic maneuvers to gain the support of Gnaeus Pompeius, either as an ally or, at the very least, to avoid his opposition. Furthermore, the candidate should cultivate and retain the support of promising young nobles, as their endorsement will add to his prestige. He has many such allies; he must ensure they understand how much he values their support. If he can inspire those who are inclined to support him to do so enthusiastically, it will be highly beneficial.
gemma3:27b.rationale: The passage is largely strategic advice, not directly related to water infrastructure or Nile Valley administration. The translation focuses on conveying the argumentative structure of Cicero’s advice, disambiguating terms like ‘publicans’ and ‘equestrian order’ to their functional meanings within Roman politics. The classification is ‘inference’ because the passage requires understanding the implicit context of a Roman political campaign to fully grasp the meaning.

author: Cornelius Nepos
title: Miltiades
original_text: Miltiades, Cimonis filius, Atheniensis, cum et antiquitate generis et gloria maiorum et sua modestia unus omnium maxime floreret eaque esset aetate, ut non iam solum de eo bene sperare, sed etiam confidere cives possent sui, talem eum futurum, qualem cognitum iudicarunt, accidit ut Athenienses Chersonesum colonos vellent mittere. cuius generis cum magnus numerus esset et multi eius demigrationis peterent societatem, ex iis delecti Delphos deliberatum missi sunt qui consulerent Apollinem , quo po

gemma3:27b.cot_english: Miltiades, son of Cimon, an Athenian, was flourishing due to his family's ancient lineage, the glory of his ancestors, and his own modesty, at an age when citizens could not only hope for good things from him but also confidently expect him to be as capable as they had already known him to be. It happened that the Athenians wanted to send colonists to Chersonesus. Because a large number of people of this type existed and many were seeking to join the migration, selected representatives were sent to Delphi to consult the god Apollo, specifically to determine which leader they should use, as the Thracians held those regions and fighting with them would be necessary. While consulting, the Pythia (the priestess of Apollo) specifically instructed them to choose Miltiades as their commander, stating that if they did so, their undertaking would be successful. Following this oracle, Miltiades set sail for Chersonesus with a select force. When he arrived near Lemnos, he wanted to bring the inhabitants of that island under Athenian control, and requested that they do so willingly. They responded with mockery, saying they would do so only when he sailed from home with ships carried by the north wind (Aquilo), because that wind blows from the north and is contrary to those sailing from Athens. Miltiades, not having time to delay, directed his course as planned and arrived at Chersonesus.
gemma3:27b.rationale: The passage is a straightforward narrative account. All statements are directly attested within the text. The translation prioritizes clarity and completeness to facilitate semantic search, resolving pronouns and providing full names for all individuals and places.

qwen3:32b.cot_english: Miltiades, son of Cimon and an Athenian, was distinguished by his family's ancient lineage, his ancestors' glory, and his personal modesty, making him the most prominent figure of his time. The Athenians, confident in his future leadership as they had judged him to be, decided to send colonists to the Chersonese region. With many seeking to join this migration, a delegation was sent to Delphi to consult the oracle of Apollo about the most suitable general for the campaign, as the Thracians controlled the area and armed conflict was expected. The Pythia explicitly advised the Athenians to appoint Miltiades as their commander, promising success if they followed this recommendation. Acting on this oracle, Miltiades led a selected force to the Chersonese. During the journey, he stopped at Lemnos to subjugate its inhabitants to Athenian authority, requesting their voluntary submission. The Lemnians mocked him, claiming they would only submit when he arrived with ships against a north wind, which typically hindered Athenian ships departing from the north. Unable to wait, Miltiades altered his course and successfully reached the Chersonese.
